file2: 2,574 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem & Common Name identifiers (Broad)
file3: 302 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem & Common Name identifiers (Broad)
file4: 1,546 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem, KEGG & Common Name identifiers (Metabolon)
file5: 611 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem & Common Name identifiers (Broad / Chen et al.)
file6: 1,314 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem & Common Name identifiers (Metabolon)
file7: 1,546 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem, KEGG & Common Name identifiers (Metabolon)
file8: 536 metabolites with HMDB, PubChem, KEGG & Common Name identifiers (Metabolon / Terunuma et al.)
file9: 163 metabolites with Common Name identifiers only (Biocrates)
file10: 309 metabolites with HMDB & Common Name identifiers (Metabolon)
MetLinkR R package source code from https://github.com/ncats/MetLinkR

data_out.description: Per-dataset mapping rate table (CSV or equivalent) listing each of the 10 files with total metabolite count and proportion successfully mapped to a RefMet standardized name

domain_knowledge: MetLinkR queries the RefMet API with a defined priority order across identifier types (HMDB, KEGG, PubChem, common name) before falling back to a RaMP-DB synonym search for unmatched identifiers.

RefMet is a standardized nomenclature for metabolites used in human metabolomics that enables cross-study comparison by resolving synonymous or platform-specific identifiers to a single canonical name.

Different metabolomics platforms (e.g., Metabolon, Broad, Biocrates) supply distinct combinations of identifier types per metabolite, making identifier heterogeneity a central challenge in meta-analysis.

A metabolite entry may carry multiple IDs (e.g., both an HMDB accession and a common name) that can resolve to different RefMet names, which MetLinkR flags as ambiguous rather than silently collapsing.

Mapping rate is calculated as the fraction of metabolites in a dataset that receive at least one valid RefMet standardized name, and serves as the primary benchmark metric for pipeline performance across the 10 files.

direct_checks: verify file 'mapping_rate_table.csv' (or equivalent structured output) exists after running the MetLinkR pipeline on all 10 supplementary dataset files — file_exists check, robust to parameter choices
verify the output table contains exactly 10 rows, one per dataset (file1 through file10) — row_count_equals 10

verify the output table contains fields for dataset identifier, total metabolite count, mapped metabolite count, and mapping rate (as proportion or percentage) — field_present for each of these four columns

verify that the metabolite count values in the output table match the reported per-file counts: file1=542, file2=2574, file3=302, file4=1546, file5=611, file6=1314, file7=1546, file8=536, file9=163, file10=309 — output_matches_reference, exact match on total counts
verify all mapping rate values in the output table are in the range [0.0, 1.0] (or [0, 100] if expressed as percentages) — value_in_range check for each row
verify the script invoking MetLinkR on all 10 dataset files runs to completion without errors using R and the ncats/MetLinkR repository at https://github.com/ncats/MetLinkR — script_runs check
verify the output file format is CSV or TSV (tabular, machine-readable) — file_format_is check
verify the output table contains the string 'RefMet' in a column header or metadata field indicating standardized name target — contains_substring 'RefMet', exact
expert_review: Assess whether the mapping rates produced by the automated pipeline are plausible given the identifier types available per dataset (e.g., file9 with only common names from Biocrates is expected to have a lower mapping rate than files with HMDB/PubChem IDs), and whether any anomalously low or high rates warrant investigation.

Assess whether the RefMet standardized names returned by the pipeline are chemically appropriate for the input identifiers, checking a sample of mappings from at least three datasets spanning different identifier types (HMDB-only, common-name-only, multi-ID).

Assess whether the handling of datasets with multiple identifier types (e.g., file1 with HMDB, KEGG, PubChem, and Common Name) follows the documented RefMet API priority order described in Figure 1A, and whether conflicts between identifier types are flagged appropriately.

cmd: library(MetLinkR); datasets <- list({inputs.0}, {inputs.1}, {inputs.2}, {inputs.3}, {inputs.4}, {inputs.5}, {inputs.6}, {inputs.7}, {inputs.8}, {inputs.9}); names(datasets) <- c('file1', 'file2', 'file3', 'file4', 'file5', 'file6', 'file7', 'file8', 'file9', 'file10'); mapping_results <- lapply(names(datasets), function(fname) { data <- read.csv(datasets[[fname]]); mapped <- metlink_map(data, id_types=c('HMDB', 'KEGG', 'PubChem', 'CommonName'), refmet_api=TRUE, ramp_fallback=TRUE); total <- nrow(mapped); success <- sum(!is.na(mapped$refmet_name)); rate <- success / total; data.frame(dataset=fname, total_metabolites=total, mapped_count=success, mapping_rate=rate) }); results_df <- do.call(rbind, mapping_results); write.csv(results_df, 'metabolite_mapping_rates_{task_id}.csv', row.names=FALSE); cat('Mapping complete. Results saved to metabolite_mapping_rates_{task_id}.csv\n'); print(results_df)

expected_outputs: Per-dataset mapping rate table (CSV or equivalent) listing each of the 10 files with total metabolite count and proportion successfully mapped to a RefMet standardized name
finding: Across the benchmarked datasets, MetLinkR achieved an 85.3% identification rate of metabolite entities relative to a manual curator, with mapping rates visualised per dataset.

methodology_summary: Load each supplementary dataset file containing heterogeneous metabolite identifiers.
Query each identifier against the RefMet API following a defined priority order of identifier types.
Fall back to a RaMP-DB synonym search for identifiers that receive no RefMet match.
Record mapping success or failure for each metabolite entry per dataset.
Aggregate results into a per-dataset mapping rate table across all 10 files.
missing_information: No explicit missing-information notes were detected.

task_description: Apply the MetLinkR identifier-mapping pipeline to each of the 10 supplementary dataset files to convert heterogeneous metabolite identifiers (HMDB, KEGG, PubChem, common names) to RefMet standardized names. Produce a per-dataset mapping rate table summarizing the proportion of metabolites successfully mapped in each file.

task_objective: Compute and tabulate per-dataset RefMet mapping rates for all 10 supplementary files using the MetLinkR pipeline.

task_outputs.label: Per-dataset mapping rate table for all 10 supplementary files

title: Map metabolite identifiers (HMDB, KEGG, PubChem, common name) to RefMet standardized names across 10 datasets
uncertainty_notes: This card was generated by the LLM-assisted pipeline and needs scientific expert review.
Each TracedClaim's evidence_span has been substring-checked against its source section; see logs/llm_calls.jsonl and capsules/<task_id>/quality_report.json for groundedness results.
workflow_description: 1. Clone or install the MetLinkR R package from https://github.com/ncats/MetLinkR. 2. Load each of the 10 supplementary dataset files (file1–file10), which contain varying combinations of HMDB, KEGG, PubChem, and common-name identifiers across 163–2,574 metabolites per file. 3. For each dataset, run the MetLinkR pipeline: first query identifiers against the RefMet API in priority order, then fall back to a RaMP-DB synonym search for any unmatched identifiers. 4. Record the number of metabolites successfully mapped to a RefMet standardized name versus total metabolites per dataset. 5. Aggregate per-dataset mapping counts into a summary table reporting mapping rates (proportion mapped) for all 10 files.
